claim: Text embeddings with 1,536 dimensions and 16-bit precision contain approximately 20 KB of compressible information per vector, sufficient to recover substantial text content when reversed through specialized models.
actionable: Use this information-theoretic bound when designing embedding security models or evaluating privacy risks of shared embeddings in vector databases.
source_title: Information Theory for Language Models: Jack Morris

claim: GPT-style language models in 32-bit precision can store approximately 3.6 bits of information per parameter through empirical measurement on random data memorization tasks.
actionable: Use the 3.6 bits-per-parameter metric as a baseline for assessing model capacity improvements and optimization techniques when designing new architectures.
source_title: Information Theory for Language Models: Jack Morris

claim: All major embedding models (BERT-based, T5-based, GPT-based) learn remarkably similar representations that can be aligned without paired training data using techniques analogous to CycleGAN.
actionable: Leverage representation alignment across different embedding models to build adapter layers that convert between model embeddings without expensive retraining.
source_title: Information Theory for Language Models: Jack Morris

claim: Pre-training works because it makes information more extractable under computational constraints—a key insight from V-information theory that explains why pre-trained weights generalize better than random initialization.
actionable: Frame pre-training effectiveness through the lens of computational extractability rather than just parameter count when designing efficient transfer learning approaches.
source_title: Information Theory for Language Models: Jack Morris

claim: You can recover 90% of text verbatim from medium-length sentence embeddings (OpenAI's model) through inversion, creating privacy risks for vector database deployments that share embeddings with users.
actionable: Treat embeddings as sensitive data equivalent to the original text; never share customer embeddings directly without encryption when deploying vector databases.
source_title: Information Theory for Language Models: Jack Morris

claim: There are no fundamentally new paradigm shifts in AI—only new datasets. Past breakthroughs (AlexNet+ImageNet, Transformers+web-scale pre-training, instruction tuning+human preference data, reasoning+verification data) all paired novel methods with novel data sources.
actionable: When evaluating AI research claims, ask first about the data source before the algorithm; prioritize finding or curating novel high-quality data over incremental architectural tweaks.
source_title: Information Theory for Language Models: Jack Morris

claim: Model weights contain recoverable information about training data distribution; given access to base and fine-tuned checkpoints, you can reconstruct approximate training sets through gradient-based selection of public corpora.
actionable: When releasing both base and fine-tuned models, be aware that competitors can approximate your fine-tuning data; consider differential privacy or releasing only the final weights.
source_title: Information Theory for Language Models: Jack Morris

claim: Graduate students working on LLM research today face disadvantages relative to industry labs due to lack of compute access for multi-node distributed training, which isn't taught in most CS programs.
actionable: As a grad student, prioritize learning distributed training frameworks (DeepSpeed, FSDP) by engaging directly with maintainers (Microsoft DeepSpeed team, PyTorch team are responsive to inquiries) rather than waiting for formal coursework.
source_title: Information Theory for Language Models: Jack Morris

claim: The research process for hard problems often involves testing 10+ approaches that fail before finding one that works; this is normal and rewarding, not a sign of poor direction.
actionable: When stuck on a research problem, embrace iterative failure as part of the process; set up regular feedback loops with advisors and use negative results to narrow the solution space.
source_title: Information Theory for Language Models: Jack Morris

claim: Information theory lacks a clear definition of 'a bit' in deep learning contexts; this is an open problem that will unlock better understanding of how models store and use information.
actionable: If you're interested in fundamentals, work on formalizing information-theoretic concepts for neural networks; this is an underexplored area with high impact potential.
source_title: Information Theory for Language Models: Jack Morris
